summary: Gruber translates Microsoft's announcement of 10 principles for the Microsoft Store on Windows, revealing the self-serving strategy behind the corporate language. He argues that Microsoft is changing nothing about its own platforms — Windows remains open as always, Xbox remains closed as always — while positioning itself to pressure Apple into opening up iOS. The piece highlights the hypocrisy of Microsoft explicitly exempting Xbox from these principles while demanding openness from Apple. Gruber points out that Microsoft's real grievance is about iOS App Store fees and restrictions on their services like Xbox Game Pass and Office subscriptions. The translation format exposes how each principle either restates the status quo on Windows or takes a shot at Apple's App Store policies.
key_points: Microsoft's 10 principles for the Microsoft Store change nothing about how Windows actually works — it has always allowed sideloading and competing stores
Microsoft explicitly exempts Xbox from these principles, despite Xbox being more closed than iOS, revealing the self-serving nature of the announcement
The real targets are Apple's App Store policies: IAP commission on Office subscriptions and the inability to ship Xbox Game Pass streaming as a single iOS app
Microsoft's justification for exempting Xbox — that consoles are 'specialized devices' sold below cost — is the same logic Apple could use for iPhone
The entire announcement is a lobbying play aimed at regulators, dressed up as principled corporate governance
money_quotes: Game consoles are general purpose computers limited to particular uses by the platform owners.
Game consoles are different because we own a successful game console platform.
We sure wish our attempts at a phone platform had taken off.
Can you even fucking believe we have to cut them in on these subscriptions?
Nothing is changing on Windows, which remains every bit as open as it always has been. Nothing is changing on Xbox, which remains every bit as closed as it always has been.

We're sure hoping government regulators will force Apple to open up iOS and treat it more like a PC platform where third-party developers are free to do what they want, because we know Apple won't do it of their own volition because we wouldn't either if we were them.
There's a lot of money at stake here and we really blew it with our attempts at a phone platform.
key_insight: Microsoft's app store principles are a lobbying strategy that changes nothing about their own platforms while demanding Apple open up iOS — a position undermined by Microsoft keeping Xbox even more locked down than the App Store.
